danger_signals.meaning: Active hostile faction in early game; visual distinction of enemy general units from player forces

danger_signals.visual_form: Generic enemy units and named generals appearing in random battles during travel
danger_signals.meaning: Unstable regions with multiple warlord factions; danger increases with territorial complexity

danger_signals.signal: Kingdom of Sun Wu and Cao Wei invasion markers
danger_signals.meaning: Late-game territorial expansion by opposing kingdoms; visual manifestation of political threat through map control

safety_signals.signal: Controlled territories under Liu Bei banner
safety_signals.visual_form: Shu Han faction visual identity applied to map regions and settlements
safety_signals.meaning: Player-controlled safe zones; reduced encounter rates with hostile generals

safety_signals.visual_form: Named character roster with up to 70 members; active combat group of 5-7 characters
safety_signals.meaning: Conversion of enemies to allies; formerly dangerous opponents become protective forces

safety_signals.visual_form: Player gathering peasants and farmers from nearby villages and camps
safety_signals.meaning: Population centers under player protection; growing military strength through recruitment

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Territories claimed under Shu Han control; peasant/farmer villages pledging allegiance

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinguishable as rebel forces in early-game encounters
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Villages under threat or rebel control; sorcerer Zhang Jiao presence indicates Yellow Turban stronghold

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Named generals of Cao Wei faction appear as distinct enemy units
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Northern/eastern territories; late-game invasion presence; visual distinction from player-controlled lands

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Named generals of Sun Wu faction appear as distinct enemy units
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Southern territories; late-game invasion presence; visual distinction from player-controlled lands

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early game: Zhang Jiao threat active
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Yellow Turban rebel forces visible in villages and travel routes; Xu Province governance unstable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Frequent encounters with rebel generals; peasants and farmers in defensive postures; villages request protection

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mid game: Liu Bei assumes Xu Province governance
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tao Qian's authority replaced by Liu Bei's banner; settlements transition to Shu Han visual identity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shift from defensive militia to active recruitment; defeated generals offer to join party; region stabilizes under player control

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late game: Multiple warlord invasions (Cao Wei and Sun Wu)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Opposing kingdom generals and forces appear across map; territorial boundaries shift; non-player faction colors/sigils expand into previously safe zones
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High-level named generals become mandatory encounters; recruitment pressure increases; military action accelerates; world map reflects multi-front conflict

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game completion: China united under Shu Han
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map shows unified Shu Han territory; enemy faction visuals disappear from controlled regions; celebration/narrative conclusion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Encounters cease in fully controlled territory; final victory state achieved; branching storyline paths complete with Liu Bei's favorable outcomes against rival kingdoms

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Random appearance of multiple general factions in same region; visual variety in enemy unit presentation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Random general encounter system activates; previously defeated generals may reappear if removed from party; warlord presence indicates unstable political control

environmental_storytelling.example: Villages and territories transition from generic/Yellow Turban visual state to Shu Han banner, signaling player's political expansion and stabilization of regions

Specific historical warlord generals appear in geographically appropriate regions, conveying faction presence and power distribution across map; their removal from random encounters after recruitment signals territorial consolidation

Defeated generals offer to join through bribery (money or horses), visually representing political negotiation and faction allegiance shifts; removed generals return as enemies, showing impermanence of control
Liu Bei gathering peasants and farmers from villages creates visible party expansion (up to 70 characters possible), representing territory's military capacity and allegiance scale
Sun Wu and Cao Wei generals appear in previously safe regions, visually demonstrating threat from multiple kingdoms and instability despite player's prior conquest

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appear as mandatory encounters; Zhang Jiao directs sorcerous forces; represent organized ideological insurgency rather than simple bandits
Appear in random battles across regions; offer to join party after defeat if payment provided; return to enemy status if removed from party; indicate regional warlord presence

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Liu Bei's core sworn brothers (Zhang Fei, Guan Yu)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Remain in party throughout; form militia foundation; foundational to faction identity; visual/mechanical representation of protagonist's primary support structure

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Cao Wei and Sun Wu generals (late game)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appear as high-level mandatory encounters; do not offer recruitment; represent final political obstacles; behavioral shift indicates shift from recruitment-based expansion to conquest-based conflict
Provides magic-like effects to all 5 active combat members; visual role distinct from fighter units; behavioral function emphasizes information/strategy hierarchy in world-state representation

notes: Destiny of an Emperor uses a unique recruitment system to visually manifest political faction dynamics: defeated enemies become allies, representing the absorption and consolidation of warlord forces under Liu Bei's banner. The game's world-state progression follows historical/narrative structure (Yellow Turban suppression → governorship → multi-kingdom conflict → unification), with faction presence and general encounter patterns serving as primary visual/behavioral indicators of regional political stability. The branching storyline mechanic communicates player agency in altering historical outcome, with visual manifestation through territory control and recruited character roster size. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on named general placement and banner/sigil transitions rather than explicit visual environmental changes, typical of NES-era technical constraints. The 150-character roster with varying power progression creates visual stratification of faction members by utility period, indicating evolving political landscape and military hierarchy.
